flashcards.q: What is a simple pendulum?
flashcards.a: A small metallic bob suspended from a rigid support by a long thread.

flashcards.q: Define time period of a pendulum.
flashcards.a: The time taken by a pendulum to complete one oscillation.

flashcards.q: What is one oscillation?
flashcards.a: The complete motion from mean position through both extremes and back to mean position.

flashcards.q: Does time period depend on the mass of the bob?
flashcards.a: No, time period is independent of the bob's mass.

flashcards.q: What does time period of a pendulum depend on?
flashcards.a: The length of the pendulum.

flashcards.q: Name four ancient time-measuring devices.
flashcards.a: Sundials, water clocks, hourglasses, candle clocks.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of time?
flashcards.a: Second (s).

flashcards.q: How many seconds are in 1 hour?
flashcards.a: 3,600 seconds.

flashcards.q: How many seconds are in 1 minute?
flashcards.a: 60 seconds.

flashcards.q: Convert 3 hours into seconds.
flashcards.a: 10,800 seconds.

flashcards.q: Define speed.
flashcards.a: Distance covered by an object in unit time.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for speed.
flashcards.a: Speed = Distance / Time.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of speed?
flashcards.a: Metre per second (m/s).

flashcards.q: Name a commonly used non-SI unit of speed.
flashcards.a: Kilometre per hour (km/h).

flashcards.q: What does 1 m/s equal in km/h?
How do you convert km/h to m/s?
flashcards.a: Divide by 3.6.

flashcards.q: How do you convert m/s to km/h?
flashcards.a: Multiply by 3.6.

flashcards.q: What does a speedometer measure?
flashcards.a: The speed of a vehicle in km/h or mph.

flashcards.q: What does an odometer measure?
flashcards.a: The total distance travelled by a vehicle in kilometres.

flashcards.q: Define velocity.
flashcards.a: Speed in a specific direction (has both magnitude and direction).

flashcards.q: Is speed a scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Scalar (has magnitude only, no direction).

flashcards.q: Is velocity a scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Vector (has both magnitude and direction).

flashcards.q: What is linear motion?
flashcards.a: Motion of an object along a straight line.

flashcards.q: Define uniform linear motion.
flashcards.a: Motion along a straight line with constant speed.

flashcards.q: Define non-uniform linear motion.
flashcards.a: Motion along a straight line with changing speed.

flashcards.q: In uniform motion, how does distance relate to time intervals?
flashcards.a: Equal distances are covered in equal time intervals.

flashcards.q: In non-uniform motion, how does distance relate to time intervals?
flashcards.a: Unequal distances are covered in equal time intervals.

flashcards.q: Who invented the pendulum clock?
flashcards.a: Christiaan Huygens in 1656.

flashcards.q: Who first observed the constant time period of pendulums?
flashcards.a: Galileo Galilei.

flashcards.q: What is isochronism?
flashcards.a: The property that a pendulum's time period remains constant regardless of the amplitude of swing.

flashcards.q: What is average speed?
flashcards.a: Total distance divided by total time taken.

flashcards.q: What is instantaneous speed?
flashcards.a: The speed of an object at a specific moment in time.

flashcards.q: Can a vehicle have constant speed but non-uniform motion?
flashcards.a: No, if speed is constant and direction is straight, motion is uniform.

flashcards.q: If a car travels 100 km in 2 hours, what is its average speed?
flashcards.a: 50 km/h.

flashcards.q: If a cyclist travels at 15 km/h for 3 hours, how far does he travel?
flashcards.a: 45 km.

flashcards.q: If a train covers 240 km in 4 hours, what is its average speed?
flashcards.a: 60 km/h.

flashcards.q: What is the formula to find distance if speed and time are known?
flashcards.a: Distance = Speed × Time.

flashcards.q: What is the formula to find time if speed and distance are known?
flashcards.a: Time = Distance / Speed.

flashcards.q: A runner covers 400 m in 50 seconds. What is the speed in m/s?
flashcards.a: 8 m/s.

flashcards.q: What is 10 m/s in km/h?
flashcards.a: 36 km/h.

flashcards.q: What is 72 km/h in m/s?
What is the Ghatika-yantra?
flashcards.a: An ancient Indian water clock (sinking bowl) that took 24 minutes to fill and sink.

flashcards.q: How many ghatikas are in a 24-hour day?
flashcards.a: 60 ghatikas (each ghatika = 24 minutes).

flashcards.q: What was the advantage of the Ghatika-yantra over simple water clocks?
flashcards.a: It maintained constant accuracy because the floating bowl kept water pressure constant.

flashcards.q: Who invented the Ghatika-yantra?
flashcards.a: Ancient Indian scientists; first mentioned by astronomer Aryabhata.

flashcards.q: What is a sundial?
flashcards.a: A device that uses the shadow of an object cast by the sun to indicate time.

flashcards.q: How much does the shadow of a sundial move per minute?
flashcards.a: Approximately 0.25 degrees (1 degree per 4 minutes).

flashcards.q: What is an hourglass?
flashcards.a: A device that measures time using sand flowing from one bulb to another.

flashcards.q: What is a water clock?
flashcards.a: A device that measures time using water flowing from one container to another.

flashcards.q: What was the main limitation of water clocks?
flashcards.a: Accuracy decreased as water level dropped because flow rate decreased.

flashcards.q: Why is a longer pendulum slower than a shorter pendulum?
flashcards.a: Longer length means longer arc to traverse, so it takes more time to complete one oscillation.

flashcards.q: If a pendulum's length is quadrupled, how does the time period change?
flashcards.a: The time period doubles (time period is proportional to sqrt(length)).

flashcards.q: What happens to a pendulum's time period at different locations on Earth?
flashcards.a: Time period changes slightly due to variation in gravitational acceleration (g varies with latitude and altitude).

flashcards.q: What is the difference between distance and displacement?
flashcards.a: Distance is total path length; displacement is the straight-line distance from start to end point.

flashcards.q: Is distance a scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Scalar (magnitude only).

flashcards.q: Is displacement a scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Vector (magnitude and direction).

flashcards.q: What is acceleration?
flashcards.a: Rate of change of velocity (how quickly speed changes).

flashcards.q: In uniform motion, what is the acceleration?
flashcards.a: Zero, because speed remains constant.

flashcards.q: In non-uniform motion, what is the acceleration?
flashcards.a: Non-zero, because speed is changing.

flashcards.q: What is positive acceleration?
flashcards.a: When velocity increases in the direction of motion.

flashcards.q: What is negative acceleration (deceleration)?
flashcards.a: When velocity decreases or increases in the opposite direction.

flashcards.q: What is relative speed?
flashcards.a: The speed of one object as observed from another moving object's reference frame.

flashcards.q: When two objects move in the same direction, how is relative speed calculated?
flashcards.a: Subtract the slower speed from the faster speed.

flashcards.q: When two objects move in opposite directions, how is relative speed calculated?
flashcards.a: Add their individual speeds.

flashcards.q: What is the most common type of motion in real-world situations?
flashcards.a: Non-uniform motion (speed changes due to various forces).

flashcards.q: Why is average speed more meaningful than instantaneous speed for describing overall motion?
flashcards.a: It provides a single representative value for the entire journey even when speed varies.

flashcards.q: What factors affect the time period of a simple pendulum?
flashcards.a: Only the length of the pendulum (for small oscillations).

flashcards.q: What factors do NOT affect the time period of a simple pendulum?
flashcards.a: Mass of the bob, amplitude of swing, material of the thread.

flashcards.q: What is the formula for the time period of a simple pendulum?
flashcards.a: T = 2π√(L/g), where L is length and g is gravitational acceleration.

flashcards.q: What does 'g' represent in the pendulum formula?
flashcards.a: Acceleration due to gravity (approximately 10 m/s² or 9.8 m/s²).

flashcards.q: Why did Galileo's observation of the swinging lamp lead to the invention of the pendulum clock?
flashcards.a: He discovered that the time period was constant, making it ideal for precise timekeeping.

flashcards.q: How much can a pendulum clock reduce error compared to mechanical clocks?
flashcards.a: Mechanical clocks: several minutes per day; Pendulum clock: about 10 seconds per day.

flashcards.q: What is the main advantage of a pendulum clock over a mechanical clock?
flashcards.a: Much greater accuracy in timekeeping due to the constant time period of pendulums.

flashcards.q: What does 'mean position' of a pendulum refer to?
flashcards.a: The equilibrium position where the pendulum hangs vertically at rest.

flashcards.q: What does 'amplitude' of a pendulum refer to?
flashcards.a: The maximum displacement from the mean position.

flashcards.q: For accurate time measurement with a pendulum, what should the amplitude be?
flashcards.a: Small (typically less than 15° for accurate time period).

flashcards.q: What is a 'complete oscillation' of a pendulum?
flashcards.a: One full back-and-forth motion starting and ending at the mean position.

flashcards.q: How many half-oscillations make one complete oscillation?
flashcards.a: Two half-oscillations make one complete oscillation.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between speed, distance, and time?
flashcards.a: Speed = Distance / Time; Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance / Speed.

flashcards.q: If speed is constant, what type of motion is it?
flashcards.a: Uniform linear motion.

flashcards.q: If speed is changing, what type of motion is it?
flashcards.a: Non-uniform linear motion.

flashcards.q: What is the practical application of knowing a vehicle's average speed?
flashcards.a: To estimate travel time and fuel consumption for long journeys.

flashcards.q: Why is it important to measure time accurately?
flashcards.a: For scientific experiments, navigation, communications, daily activities, and many technological applications.

flashcards.q: What modern device uses atomic timekeeping?
flashcards.a: GPS (Global Positioning System), smartphones, atomic clocks for scientific standards.

flashcards.q: How much error does an atomic clock have per year?
flashcards.a: Approximately 1 second error in millions of years (extremely precise).

flashcards.q: How much error did early mechanical clocks have?
flashcards.a: Several minutes per day.

flashcards.q: How much error did a pendulum clock have?
flashcards.a: About 10 seconds per day (150 times more accurate than mechanical clocks).

flashcards.q: What is a 'degree' in angular measurement?
flashcards.a: 1/360th of a complete circle.

flashcards.q: How many degrees does the Earth rotate in 1 hour?
flashcards.a: 360° / 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour.

flashcards.q: How many degrees does the Earth rotate in 1 minute?
flashcards.a: 15° / 60 minutes = 0.25 degrees per minute.

flashcards.q: What does the speed of light indicate about distance and time?
flashcards.a: Light covers 300,000 km in 1 second demonstrating the relationship between distance and time at extreme speeds.
